preFix.title: Almond brittle
preFix.subtitle: Caramel and toasted almond toffee, very thin
preFix.excerpt: Almond brittle is a thin sheet of amber caramel set with toasted flaked almonds, poured onto a tray and left to harden. It snaps cleanly into irregular shards. The process is fast: the sugar cooks in under 10 minutes. The most dangerous moment is pouring the hot caramel, which should be done steadily and without hesitation.
preFix.sourceNotes: Nut brittles appear across European and American confectionery traditions from at least the eighteenth century. British toffees and pralines are closely related; the word brittle appears in American confectionery books from the nineteenth century to describe the thinner, less chewy version. Almond brittle specifically is documented in British domestic cookery as a petits-fours or teatime sweet. The technique of cooking sugar to hard crack stage before adding nuts is consistent across all regional versions.

content.text: Brittle is one of the simplest confections to make, but the hot caramel reaches above 150 degrees C and will cause serious burns if it contacts skin. Keep children away from the stove at this stage, and use a long-handled spoon. Line the tray before you start: once the almonds go in, the mixture thickens quickly and needs to be poured within 30 seconds. A silicone baking mat gives a perfectly flat, easy-release sheet, but well-greased baking paper works equally well.

Line a large baking tray with a silicone mat or lightly oiled baking paper. Toast the flaked almonds in a dry frying pan over medium heat until pale gold, then set aside. Combine 200 g caster sugar and 4 tablespoons of water in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring and increase the heat. Cook until the temperature reaches 155 degrees C on a sugar thermometer and the caramel is a deep amber colour.

Remove from the heat and immediately stir in the toasted almonds and a quarter teaspoon of salt. Pour at once onto the prepared tray and tip the tray gently so the caramel spreads in a thin, even layer. Do not spread with a spoon: this causes the caramel to set in ridges. Leave to harden completely at room temperature for 20 minutes. Snap into shards with the back of a spoon or your hands.

items.problem: The caramel crystallised and turned grainy before reaching temperature
items.solution: Stirring after the sugar dissolved caused crystallisation. Once the sugar is dissolved, do not stir, swirl, or agitate the pan. If crystallisation starts, add a tablespoon of water and a few drops of lemon juice and reheat gently.

items.problem: The brittle is sticky rather than snapping cleanly
items.solution: The caramel did not reach 155 degrees C. Below 150 degrees C the brittle will not set hard. Use a thermometer rather than guessing by colour.
